slides.title: Four Ways to Handle a Risk
slides.subtitle: Cyber Foundations: once a risk is identified and assessed, an organization has exactly four options — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept — and the choice is a business decision, not just a technical one
slides.footerLeft: AP Cybersecurity · College Board CED (Effective Fall 2026)
slides.script: Lesson 2.1, Day 5 of 8. The last few days built the risk picture: you can identify adversaries, trace the phases of an attack, and run the risk assessment process — figuring out which assets are exposed, how likely an attack is, and how severe the damage would be. Today, learning objective 2.1.E, is what an organization actually does with that information. The College Board is precise here: once a risk has been identified and assessed, an organization has exactly four options for managing it — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept. That is the whole list. Today you'll learn what each one means, when each is possible, and why this is a business decision about cost and mission, not only a technical one. Tomorrow, 2.1.F, is the security controls that mitigation actually uses.

slides.prompt: Your school runs an online store that sells spirit wear. A risk assessment finds a real vulnerability in the checkout page that could expose customer card numbers. The store brings in modest revenue, the fix is expensive, and buying cyber-insurance is an option.
slides.subprompt: Write 3–5 sentences: list every different way you can think of that the school could 'deal with' this risk. Don't just say 'fix it' — what other choices does the school have?
slides.directions: Independent writing. I will cold-call 2–3 students.
slides.script: Two minutes, on your own. Your school's spirit-wear store has a checkout vulnerability that could leak customer card numbers. The store makes modest money, the fix is expensive, and insurance is available. In three to five sentences, list every distinct way the school could deal with this risk — and push past 'just fix it.' What other choices exist? Hold your answer. By the end of class you'll know that the College Board says there are exactly four options, and you'll be able to name and apply each one. This is EK 2.1.E.1.

slides.heading: What You’ll Be Filling In
slides.subheading: Your Day 5 Guided Notes have two sections that match today’s lesson. Both are CB-required.

sections.title: The Four Risk-Management Options
sections.description: Once a risk is identified and assessed, an organization can avoid, transfer, mitigate, or accept it (EK 2.1.E.1–E.4).

sections.title: Residual Risk and Cost-Effectiveness
sections.description: Acceptance handles the residual risk that remains, and organizations favor cost-effective, easy-to-maintain solutions (EK 2.1.E.5, E.6).

slides.note: Fill in your Guided Notes as we go. The Lesson 2.1 page on apcsexamprep.com has every answer if you fall behind.
slides.script: Two sections today, both CB-required. First, the four risk-management options themselves — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept — what each means and when each is even possible. Second, the two ideas that make the framework realistic: residual risk, the leftover risk you accept after doing everything else, and cost-effectiveness, the reason organizations don't just mitigate everything. Keep the packet open; I collect Guided Notes at the end.

slides.heading: By the End of Today You Will...

items.text: Name the four risk-management strategies — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept — and what each one actually does (LO 2.1.E)
Pick the right strategy from a scenario and explain why avoidance is impossible for a mission-critical activity (LO 2.1.E)
Define residual risk and use the cost-effectiveness test to judge when accepting or transferring beats mitigating (LO 2.1.E)

slides.script: Three things to leave with today, all under learning objective 2.1.E, identify strategies for managing risk. First, name the four strategies — avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept — and know what each does: avoidance stops the risky activity, transference shifts the burden to an entity like an insurer, mitigation adds controls to shrink the risk, and acceptance is the deliberate choice to live with what's left. Second, pick the right strategy from a scenario, and explain why avoidance is off the table when the activity is critical to the mission. Third, define residual risk and use the cost-effectiveness test — favor controls that cost less than the expected loss — to judge when accepting or transferring beats mitigating. By the end of today you can name all four and apply them.

slides.label: The Four Options
slides.script: Section five of the lesson. Learning objective 2.1.E. Four options for managing a risk, and only four. As we go, listen for what makes each one possible — or impossible. Twelve minutes here.

slides.heading: Managing a Risk: Three of the Four Strategies in Action
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.E.2, E.3, and E.4 — avoid, transfer, mitigate. Acceptance (E.5) handles whatever these leave behind.

items.title: Avoid the Risk
items.summary: Risk avoidance stops the activity that is generating the risk altogether. It is the most complete option, but if the activity is a critical part of the organization's mission or purpose, then avoidance is simply not possible.

items.title: Transfer the Risk
items.summary: Risk transference places the burden of the risk on another entity — such as an insurance company, a government, or consumers. The activity continues, but someone else absorbs the cost if the risk is realized.

items.title: Mitigate the Risk
items.summary: Risk mitigation implements security controls to reduce the likelihood or impact of a risk. The activity continues and the organization keeps the risk, but controls shrink how often it happens or how badly it hurts.

slides.script: Three of the four strategies, side by side. First, EK 2.1.E.2, avoid: you stop the activity generating the risk entirely — no checkout page, no card-theft risk. It's the cleanest option, but read the catch the College Board states: if the activity is critical to the organization's mission, avoidance isn't possible. A hospital can't avoid storing patient records. Second, EK 2.1.E.3, transfer: the activity continues, but you push the burden onto another entity — an insurance company, a government, or consumers. Buying cyber-insurance is the textbook example. Third, EK 2.1.E.4, mitigate: you keep doing the activity and you keep the risk, but you add security controls to reduce its likelihood or its impact. Write all three with their EK numbers. The fourth option, accept, is next — and it's different in kind.

slides.heading: The Fourth Option: Accept the Residual Risk
slides.subheading: EK 2.1.E.5 — after avoiding, transferring, and mitigating, something is always left over. Accepting it is a deliberate choice.

left.title: What Residual Risk Is (EK 2.1.E.5)
left.definition: The risk that remains after everything else
examples: Residual risk is what remains after avoidance, transference, and mitigation
It is the level of risk an organization is willing to accept
Risk acceptance acknowledges that absolute security is unattainable
Accepting is an active decision, not ignoring the risk

right.title: Why Acceptance Is Necessary
right.definition: You can never drive risk to zero
examples: No control stops every attack; some exposure always survives
Eliminating every risk would cost more than the organization could bear
So the organization names a level of leftover risk it can live with
Acceptance is the conscious choice to operate at that level

slides.footer: AP TIP: Acceptance is not 'doing nothing.' It is the deliberate decision to live with the RESIDUAL risk (EK 2.1.E.5) that remains after the other three strategies — because absolute security is unattainable.
slides.script: The fourth option, acceptance, deserves its own slide because students get it wrong. On the left, EK 2.1.E.5 defines residual risk: the risk that remains after an organization has gone through avoidance, transference, and mitigation. That leftover is the level of risk the organization is willing to accept — and risk acceptance is the acknowledgment that absolute security is unattainable. On the right is why acceptance has to exist: no control blocks every attack, and trying to eliminate every last risk would cost more than any organization could bear. So you name a level you can live with and you consciously accept it. The exam trap: acceptance is NOT ignoring the risk or doing nothing. It's the deliberate decision to operate with the residual risk after you've done everything cost-effective. Write residual risk and risk acceptance with EK 2.1.E.5.

slides.heading: The ‘Always Mitigate Everything’ Myth
slides.misconception: A good security team should mitigate every risk it finds — accepting a risk means the team failed to do its job.
slides.whyItHappens: Mitigation feels like the 'real' security work — adding controls, fixing flaws — so it's tempting to treat the other three strategies, especially acceptance, as cop-outs or as evidence that something was missed.
slides.correction: EK 2.1.E.5 says absolute security is unattainable and that residual risk always remains, so acceptance is a required part of the framework, not a failure. And EK 2.1.E.6 says organizations favor solutions that are cost-effective and easy to implement and maintain — a cost-effective solution costs less to install and maintain than the expected loss from an attack. Mitigating a tiny risk with an expensive control can be the wrong call; sometimes accept or transfer is the right strategy.
slides.example: The spirit-wear store risk: spending fifty thousand dollars to fully mitigate a vulnerability that, realistically, exposes a few hundred dollars of expected loss per year fails the cost-effectiveness test in EK 2.1.E.6. The school might mitigate the cheap parts, transfer the rest with insurance (E.3), and consciously accept the small residual risk (E.5). All four strategies are tools; the skill is matching the strategy to the cost and the mission.
slides.script: This is the misconception the exam loves. Students assume the only 'real' answer is mitigate-everything, and that accepting a risk means the team dropped the ball. But read the standard. EK 2.1.E.5 states flatly that absolute security is unattainable and residual risk always remains — so acceptance isn't a failure, it's built into the framework. And EK 2.1.E.6 adds the money lens: organizations favor solutions that are cost-effective and easy to maintain, where cost-effective means the control costs less than the expected loss it prevents. So pouring fifty thousand dollars into mitigating a few-hundred-dollar-a-year risk is actually the wrong decision. The right answer might be to mitigate the cheap fixes, transfer the rest with insurance, and accept the small remainder. The skill the College Board tests is matching the strategy to cost and mission — not always reaching for mitigation.

slides.heading: Key Vocabulary (LO 2.1.E)

terms.definition: A risk-management strategy that stops the activity generating the risk entirely; not possible when the activity is critical to the organization's mission (EK 2.1.E.2).
A risk-management strategy that places the burden of the risk on another entity, such as an insurance company, a government, or consumers (EK 2.1.E.3).
A risk-management strategy that implements security controls to reduce the likelihood or impact of a risk while the activity continues (EK 2.1.E.4).
The deliberate decision to live with the residual risk that remains, acknowledging that absolute security is unattainable (EK 2.1.E.5).
The risk that remains after an organization has gone through avoidance, transference, and mitigation; the level of risk it is willing to accept (EK 2.1.E.5).
A solution that costs less to install and maintain than the expected loss from an attack, favored because it conserves financial resources and employee capacity (EK 2.1.E.6).

slides.script: Six terms, all on LO 2.1.E. The four strategies are the spine: avoidance stops the activity (E.2), transference shifts the burden to another entity (E.3), mitigation adds controls to shrink the risk (E.4), and acceptance is the deliberate choice to live with what's left (E.5). Then two supporting ideas the exam uses to make scenarios realistic: residual risk, the leftover after the first three (E.5), and cost-effective solution, one that costs less than the expected loss it prevents (E.6). Get all six into your notes with EK numbers — and be able to tell avoidance from mitigation, because that's the pair students confuse.

slides.heading: Stop and Think — Independent Writing
slides.subheading: All questions map to LO 2.1.E. Complete on your Guided Notes packet.
prompts: A company decides to shut down its little-used customer forum because it keeps getting attacked and isn't core to the business. Name the risk-management strategy, and explain why this option was available here but would NOT be available for the company's main payment system.

A hospital cannot stop storing patient records, so it installs encryption and access controls, buys cyber-insurance, and documents the small remaining exposure it chooses to live with. Identify all THREE risk-management strategies in this scenario with their numbers, and explain what 'residual risk' refers to.
slides.directions: Independent. 6 minutes. Full sentences. I will collect Guided Notes at the end of class.
slides.script: Two questions, six minutes, independent, full sentences. Question one: the company shuts down the forum it doesn't need — name the strategy, that's avoidance, EK 2.1.E.2, and explain why avoidance works here but not for the payment system the company can't live without. Question two is the integration question: the hospital encrypts and adds access controls, buys insurance, and documents what it chooses to live with — identify all three strategies, mitigation, transference, and acceptance, with EK numbers, and define the residual risk it's accepting. Be precise about which strategy is which.

slides.eyebrow: Your Turn — It's All on the Site

items.tail: Match avoid / transfer / mitigate / accept to cases.

Re-run the Lesson 2.1 self-check, then work Exercise 1, where you name the risk-management strategy in each scenario, and Exercise 2, where you match avoid, transfer, mitigate, and accept to a set of cases. If you want today again at your own pace, the lesson video walks the material slide for slide. Write the lesson page URL down now.

slides.heading: End of Day 5
todayPoints: Named the four CB-specified strategies for managing risk: avoid, transfer, mitigate, accept (LO 2.1.E)
Defined each: avoidance stops the activity (E.2), transference shifts the burden (E.3), mitigation adds controls (E.4)
Explained residual risk and risk acceptance — absolute security is unattainable (E.5)
Applied the cost-effectiveness lens: favor controls that cost less than the expected loss (E.6)
nextPoints: Learn the types of security controls that mitigation actually uses (LO 2.1.F)
Classify controls by type — managerial, operational, technical, physical — and by function
See how preventive, detective, and corrective controls each do a different job
slides.teaser: Today, mitigation meant 'add security controls' — but what exactly is a security control? Tomorrow you'll learn the categories the College Board uses to classify every one of them, from a locked door to an encryption key.
slides.script: Day 5 done. You can name all four risk-management strategies and apply them: avoid stops the activity, transfer shifts the burden to another entity, mitigate adds controls, and accept is the deliberate choice to live with the residual risk because absolute security is unattainable. And you can use the cost-effectiveness test — favor solutions that cost less than the expected loss. Tomorrow, EK 2.1.F: we open up mitigation and look at the actual security controls — how the College Board classifies every one, by type and by function. Review your Guided Notes — be able to name all four strategies, their EK numbers, and which one isn't available when an activity is mission-critical.
